Commands to run inside this container (no sudo required)
- Purpose: confirm we are in a container and show container process / cgroup info.
```bash
# confirm PID 1 and container clues (short)

cat /proc/1/cgroup 2>/dev/null || echo "/proc/1/cgroup not available"
```

- Purpose: show a compact process list (avoid huge output)
```bash
ps aux --width 120 --sort=-%mem | head -n 30
```

- Purpose: show small sample of logs available inside container (if any)
```bash
ls -lah /var/log 2>/dev/null | head -n 50
# tail the last 200 lines of any likely log file (adjust filename if needed)
for f in /var/log/syslog /var/log/messages /var/log/*.log; do
  [ -f "$f" ] || continue
  echo "== $f =="; tail -n 200 "$f"
done
```

- Purpose: check disk usage concisely (already saw overlay), but give a small report
```bash
df -hT /  --output=source,fstype,size,used,avail,pcent,target
df -h --total --output=source,size,used,avail,pcent | head -n 20
```

- Purpose: show mounted overlay details
```bash
mount | grep -E 'overlay|zfs' || mount | head -n 50
```

If you want dmesg / systemctl / host-level logs
- Explanation: dmesg fails inside an unprivileged container (needs CAP_SYSLOG) and systemctl won’t exist inside most minimal containers. To get kernel logs or systemd service state you must run the commands on the host (or run the container with extra privileges).
- If you have access to the host, run these on the host (they require root; keep outputs short with -n/--no-pager and head/tail):
```bash
# on the host (use sudo as needed)
sudo dmesg | tail -n 100
sudo journalctl -xe --no-pager -n 200
sudo systemctl --failed --no-pager
sudo df -hT --total | head -n 50
```

If the issue is with a Docker container and you can access the Docker host
- Purpose: inspect container and its logs (short outputs)
```bash
# on host where docker runs
docker ps -a --format '{{.ID}} {{.Names}} {{.Status}}' | head -n 50
# get recent logs from the container (replace <container> with id/name)
docker logs --tail 500 <container> 2>&1 | sed -n '1,200p'
docker inspect --format '{{.State}} {{.Config.Cmd}}' <container>
```

If you need kernel logs from inside the container specifically
- Option A (if you control how the container is started): start container with the capability to read dmesg:
  - Add capability CAP_SYSLOG: docker run --cap-add=SYSLOG ...
  - Or run privileged (not recommended for production).
- Option B: get logs from the host (preferred) as shown above.

Likely immediate causes and quick checks
- Permission / capability limits: "dmesg: Operation not permitted" is expected in unprivileged container; not an error in itself.
- Missing systemd/systemctl: many containers are minimal and do not run systemd; use process logs or the container runtime for service control.
- Disk pressure: overlay at 84% — not critical yet, but worth monitoring. If you suspect disk-full errors, run df -i and check inode usage as well:
```bash
df -i --total | head -n 20
```

Reasoning and analysis
- From the output you pasted I can already see a few useful facts:

  - The filesystem is an overlay on top of ZFS; root overlay is ~84% used (4.4T total, 3.7T used).
  - The environment looks like a container (PID 1 is tail -f /dev/null, systemctl/sudo are missing, dmesg returns "Operation not permitted").
  - You are inside a restricted container without privileges to read kernel logs or run systemd commands.
  - The earlier diagnostic command produced huge output and was truncated; that’s why you saw the “output too long” warning and return code 2.

I can’t fix anything directly from here, but I can tell you what to check and give safe, targeted commands to run (they produce small outputs). Run the relevant commands below, paste their outputs here, and tell me the exact symptom you’re trying to solve (error message, failing service, crash, etc.). I explain why I ask for each command.

Commands to run inside this container (no sudo required)
- Purpose: confirm we are in a container and show container process / cgroup info.
```bash
# confirm PID 1 and container clues (short)
ps -o pid,ppid,cmd -p 1
cat /proc/1/cgroup 2>/dev/null || echo "/proc/1/cgroup not available"
```

- Purpose: show a compact process list (avoid huge output)
```bash
ps aux --width 120 --sort=-%mem | head -n 30
```

- Purpose: show small sample of logs available inside container (if any)
```bash
ls -lah /var/log 2>/dev/null | head -n 50
# tail the last 200 lines of any likely log file (adjust filename if needed)
for f in /var/log/syslog /var/log/messages /var/log/*.log; do
  [ -f "$f" ] || continue
  echo "== $f =="; tail -n 200 "$f"
done
```

- Purpose: check disk usage concisely (already saw overlay), but give a small report
```bash
df -hT /  --output=source,fstype,size,used,avail,pcent,target
df -h --total --output=source,size,used,avail,pcent | head -n 20
```

- Purpose: show mounted overlay details
```bash
mount | grep -E 'overlay|zfs' || mount | head -n 50
```

If you want dmesg / systemctl / host-level logs
- Explanation: dmesg fails inside an unprivileged container (needs CAP_SYSLOG) and systemctl won’t exist inside most minimal containers. To get kernel logs or systemd service state you must run the commands on the host (or run the container with extra privileges).
- If you have access to the host, run these on the host (they require root; keep outputs short with -n/--no-pager and head/tail):
```bash
# on the host (use sudo as needed)
sudo dmesg | tail -n 100
sudo journalctl -xe --no-pager -n 200
sudo systemctl --failed --no-pager
sudo df -hT --total | head -n 50
```

If the issue is with a Docker container and you can access the Docker host
- Purpose: inspect container and its logs (short outputs)
```bash
# on host where docker runs
docker ps -a --format '{{.ID}} {{.Names}} {{.Status}}' | head -n 50
# get recent logs from the container (replace <container> with id/name)
docker logs --tail 500 <container> 2>&1 | sed -n '1,200p'
docker inspect --format '{{.State}} {{.Config.Cmd}}' <container>
```

If you need kernel logs from inside the container specifically
- Option A (if you control how the container is started): start container with the capability to read dmesg:
  - Add capability CAP_SYSLOG: docker run --cap-add=SYSLOG ...
  - Or run privileged (not recommended for production).
- Option B: get logs from the host (preferred) as shown above.

Likely immediate causes and quick checks
- Permission / capability limits: "dmesg: Operation not permitted" is expected in unprivileged container; not an error in itself.
- Missing systemd/systemctl: many containers are minimal and do not run systemd; use process logs or the container runtime for service control.
- Disk pressure: overlay at 84% — not critical yet, but worth monitoring. If you suspect disk-full errors, run df -i and check inode usage as well:
```bash
df -i --total | head -n 20
```

SKILLS FOR FIXING BUGS IN /testbed (bleve repo)

1) Always obey the “one action” rule
- Every response must contain EXACTLY ONE command inside a single ```bash block.
- Put any explanation OUTSIDE the code block.
- If you need multiple steps, do them across multiple turns: one command per turn.
- If you are done, output ONLY:
  echo COMPLETE_TASK_AND_SUBMIT_FINAL_OUTPUT

2) Don’t do host/system debugging in this environment
- Assume you are inside an unprivileged container (no sudo/systemctl/dmesg).
- Don’t propose “run on the host” commands; focus on repo code + tests.
- If a command needs sudo/systemd, skip it and use repo-level tooling instead.

3) Keep command output small to avoid truncation
- Prefer targeted commands:
  - Use head/tail/sed -n 'start,endp'
  - Add | head -n 50 (or sed -n '1,200p') to long producers (go test output can be huge).
- When searching, use selective patterns:
  - git grep "ExactSymbol" -- path/
  - rg -n "pattern" path/ (if ripgrep exists)
- If you must capture full output, redirect to a file then view slices:
  cmd > /tmp/out.txt 2>&1; sed -n '1,200p' /tmp/out.txt

4) Start every bugfix by reproducing with tests (Go repo)
- From /testbed:
  - go test ./... (first pass; if too long, narrow it)
  - If failure indicates a specific package: go test ./path/to/pkg -run TestName -count=1
  - Re-run only failing tests during iteration to save time.

5) Use the fastest code navigation patterns (Go)
- Identify failing file/line from test output, then:
  - sed -n 'LINE1,LINE2p' file.go
- Locate symbols precisely:
  - git grep -n "func (.*) Name" -n
  - git grep -n "Name(" path/
- See call sites:
  - git grep -n "Name(" | head

6) Understand bleve repo layout (common hotspots)
- analysis/      : analyzers, token filters, language processing
- mapping/       : index mappings, field definitions, vector mapping
- index/         : index implementations, storage integration
- search/        : queries, searchers, collectors, KNN/search logic
- document/      : document model, field types
- cmd/           : CLI utilities
When a bug mentions query/search behavior, start in search/ and mapping/.

7) Debugging strategy for behavior bugs
- Reduce to one failing test case and read it first.
- Confirm expected behavior from test assertions and comments.
- Trace flow by following the types/functions used in the test:
  Query -> Searcher -> Collector/Scoring -> Results
- Add minimal logging only if needed and remove before final patch (prefer tests over logs).

8) Editing patterns for safe Go changes
- Keep changes minimal and localized.
- After edits:
  - gofmt the touched files (gofmt -w file.go)
  - Ensure imports are correct (gofmt fixes many issues).
- Prefer early returns and explicit error handling; match existing style.

9) Avoid common shell pitfalls seen in failures
- Don’t nest bash -lc inside bash -lc unless necessary.
- Avoid complex quoting, subshell grouping, and parentheses in single commands.
- Prefer simple, linear commands; if you need variables, keep them simple and quote paths.

10) Verification checklist before finalizing
- Run targeted tests for the changed area, then run go test ./... if feasible.
- Ensure gofmt ran and git diff looks intentional (git diff).
- Confirm no debug prints left behind.

```
